data.title: Politics And The Markets 08/30/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: ClearBridge Large Cap Value Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: U.S. equities bounced back from a first-quarter correction and survived tariff and growth scares as well as geopolitical concerns to deliver solid gains in the second quarter of 2025. The rebound following April's tariff-induced Liberation Day selloff was marked by a return to AI market leadership.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in the IT and communication services sectors was beneficial.
  • Detractors: Stock selection in the industrials, health care, energy, consumer staples and consumer discretionary sectors and a health care overweight weighed on relative returns.
  • Outlook: The strong recovery, in particular for momentum stocks, following the "Liberation Day" tariff selloff has widened the valuation disparity not only between growth and value stocks but also between the "sainted few" and the rest of the value universe to levels not seeing in decades. We believe such valuation gaps are unlikely to persist over the long term.

Performance Review

  • U.S. equities bounced back from a first-quarter correction and survived tariff and growth scares as well as geopolitical concerns to deliver solid gains in the second quarter of 2025.
  • The rebound following April's tariff-induced Liberation Day selloff was marked by a return to AI market leadership, with hyperscalers showing their commitment to high levels of Al-driven capex. Semiconductor companies also benefited amid solid earnings and renewed expectations of widespread adoption of Al.
  • Renewed enthusiasm for AI buildouts has benefited Broadcom tremendously as its custom-designed chips have continued to gain broader adoption with hyperscalers as an alternative to Nvidia (NVDA)'s dominant graphics processing units in Al applications. Broadcom shares also rallied with the reduction of retaliatory tariffs between the U.S. and China, which had weighed on semiconductor manufacturers.
  • Elsewhere within IT, the portfolio benefited from the strong financial results of Microchip Technology, a leading manufacturer of analog semiconductors and microcontrollers used for automotive, industrial, consumer electronics and aerospace and defense applications. After the recent return of long-tenured CEO Steve Sanghi, Microchip has gained momentum in its early turnaround efforts while also benefiting from a long-overdue cyclical rebound.
  • Stock-specific headwinds led to overall underperformance in the quarter, however, especially within health care and industrials.
  • UnitedHealth Group (UNH) continued to underperform as higher utilization rates, primarily among its Medicare Advantage business, weighed on its insurance and provider network businesses. Concerns of poor execution were exacerbated by the resignation of its CEO and federal lawsuits against pharmacy benefit managers such as UnitedHealth's OptumRx.
  • In industrials, we are positioned away from some high-flying AI power-related stocks and a narrow group of historically highmultiple stocks which led to relative underperformance during the quarter. Strong performance of less-than-truckload freight company XPO (XPO), diversified industrial and aerospace company Parker Hannifin (PH) and agricultural equipment maker Deere failed to make up for the portfolio's AI underweight in the sector.

Outlook

  • We recognize we are in period of elevated geopolitical and policy uncertainty, and during such periods raised perceptions of risk can shorten the time horizon over which investors make decisions. We caution against doing this, however, and stick to our process of finding best-in-class franchises trading at attractive valuations that can offer compelling, risk-adjusted returns over the long haul. We continue to position the portfolio for all macro environments, prioritizing stock-level decisions as opposed to those based on factor bets or macroeconomic predictions.
  • The strong recovery, in particular for momentum stocks, following the "Liberation Day" tariff selloff has widened the valuation disparity not only between growth and value stocks but also between the "sainted few" and the rest of the value universe to levels not seeing in decades. We believe such valuation gaps are unlikely to persist over the long term. Value has historically recovered following periods of sizeable (-25%) underperformance versus growth, this threshold was triggered during the first quarter of 2025.
  • Thus, we view the current market opportunity to be particularly favorable for disciplined long-term-focused stock pickers with a focus on value stocks, and we continue to use the opportunity to recycle capital from stocks that have benefited from recent factor-based trends into high-quality companies that we expect to do well over the coming years.

Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit Mutual Funds | ETFs | Insights. Gross expenses are the fund's total annual operating expenses as of the fund's prospectus available at the time of publication. Net expenses are capped under a contractual agreement, which cannot be terminated prior to 12/31/2026 without Board consent. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns. Maximum sales charges have been updated as of August 15, 2022, please refer the Fund's prospectus for more information.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Equity securities are subject to price fluctuation and possible loss of principal. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. Short selling is a speculative strategy. Unlike the possible loss on a security that is purchased, there is no limit on the amount of loss on an appreciating security that is sold short. Derivative instruments can be illiquid, may disproportionately increase losses, and have a potentially large impact on performance. The investment style may become out of favor, which may have a negative impact on performance. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance (ESG) criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identified or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund's prospectus.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager's assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund's portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The S&P 500 Index features 500 leading U.S. publicly traded companies, with a primary emphasis on market capitalization. Source: © S&P Dow (DOW) Jones Indices LLC. All rights reserved. The Russell 1000 Value Index measures the performance of the large-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. Source: FTSE.

data.title: FDG: Nontransparent ETF With Quality Holdings But High Beta, Unconvincing Returns
data.description:
Continuing the series of articles on ETFs favoring high-beta high achievers, today I would like to initiate coverage of the American Century Focused Dynamic Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:FDG), the active nontransparent exchange-traded fund, with a Hold rating.

FDG, precisely like most concentrated growth-heavy portfolios, is having a moment this year.

However, the fact that this ETF is actively and quite successfully harnessing the overoptimistic market narrative now is not enough for me to join the bullish camp.

The issue here is that the long-term picture, which I prioritize over short-term results that in most cases tend to be short-lived and evaporate quickly with the sentiment change, is not that convincing, as since its inception in 2020, FDG has underperformed the iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF), the ETF that tracks its benchmark, while also trailing the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ), even though it has beaten the market proxied with the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV).

Undeniably, there is something to appreciate about its factor mix, as it offers exposure to some impressive growth stories (though there are nuances related to the fund's nontransparent structure and data availability). Nevertheless, it is contrary to my methodology to base an investment opinion on a factor mix only, neglecting past performance.

So below, I would like to explain why FDG is worth following, but a Buy rating is excessive.

FDG Strategy Discussion

In the fact sheet (as of June 30, 2025) available on the FDG website, the ETF is described as

...an actively managed, concentrated portfolio of large-cap growth companies with long term capital appreciation potential.

As explained in the summary prospectus,

...the portfolio managers seek securities of companies whose earnings or revenues are not only growing, but growing at an accelerated pace. This includes companies whose growth rates are expected to accelerate relative to factors including recent trends, market expectations, peers and historical data.

As to the number of holdings,

The fund normally invests in a relatively limited number of companies, generally 30 to 45 securities, but may incorporate more securities to account for liquidity constraints.

My point here is that a high-conviction minimalist approach (especially when it comes to high-beta portfolios like FDG's) can be beneficial in a buoyant market. It helps to get more out of a bullish environment and capture much more than 100% of the market's upside (i.e., FDG captured 125.4% of IVV's upside during May 2020–July 2025).

But when the sentiment turns sour, inadequate risk dispersion can lead to quite depressing performance—in 2022, FDG tumbled by 35.74% after rising by 8.52% in 2021. So with volatility that high, long-term results look quite bleak, and I will discuss this in greater depth shortly.

FDG is an ANT ETF

An essential fact investors should not overlook is that FDG is an active nontransparent ETF (frequently abbreviated as just ANT).

The gist is that it does not publish its detailed holdings every day like fully transparent ETFs do and shares its portfolio on a quarterly basis only. For instance, the most recent version available as of writing this article on August 29 is the portfolio as of June 30.

FDG does publish a "proxy portfolio" daily. Even though it should have some overlap with the actual one, they are not identical:

The ETF will publish on their website each day a "Proxy Portfolio" designed to help trading in shares of the ETF. While the Proxy Portfolio includes some of the ETF's holdings, it is not the ETF's actual portfolio.

On the one hand, utilizing the openness of ETFs that do share their holdings daily, traders can blatantly copy managers' top ideas, which, in a worst-case scenario, could essentially nullify their investment theses and dent a portfolio's return, making alpha unachievable, and FDG is protected from that.

But there are obvious risks and disadvantages too. For instance, as explained by FDG:

You may have to pay more money to trade the ETFs' shares. These ETFs will provide less information to traders, who tend to charge more for trades when they have less information.

This also creates obvious challenges for ETF researchers, as they have to rely on dated holdings in the absence of a fresh version of the portfolio.

In this regard, in the factor analysis section, I will have to make certain compromises and use the June quarter portfolio holdings and financial data as of August 29, which means conclusions are not necessarily fully accurate.

FDG Performance

When the market narrative is all about long-duration equities, the growth factor combined with high beta can deliver outstanding gains. And this is exactly what has been happening with FDG this year. However, long-term results should always be dissected with all due attention. And here, we see a few weaknesses.

As per the summary prospectus, FDG is benchmarked against the Russell 1000 Growth index, so it is logical to take a look at how its performance since inception in March 2020 compares to that of IWF, the ETF tracking this index. Also, I would like to add QQQ, the U.S. tech equity barometer (though it is not only about tech), the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF™ (SCHG), and IVV as a market proxy.

So since April 2, 2020 (FDG was incepted on March 31, but historical prices are available starting from that date), this focused growth ETF has underperformed the vehicle that tracks its benchmark, QQQ, and SCHG. It did beat the S&P 500 ETF though, but there are nuances related to risk-adjusted returns.

Next, let us look at the annualized data for the May 2020–July 2025 period (IWF was selected as a benchmark for the group).

So over that period, FDG's results look quite lackluster.

  • Its annualized return was one of the lowest in the group.
  • It outperformed IVV, though its risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe and Sortino ratios) were lower owing to its volatility.
  • Other metrics like maximum drawdown and especially the downside capture ratio also do not speak in its favor.

FDG vs. Large Growth ETFs

At the same time, compared to the Large Growth subclass, FDG looks quite strong, depending on the period assessed.

Metric FDG Median for the subclass
1M Price Return 3.04% 1.72%
6M Price Return 18.18% 13.56%
YTD Price Return 12.58% 12.50%
1Y Price Return 30.63% 24.32%
3Y Price Return 96.52% 81.69%
3Y Total Return 96.52% 85.30%
5Y Price Return 74.00% 89.39%
5Y Total Return 74.02% 95.88%

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha

For instance, its one- and six-month, as well as YTD, one- and three-year returns are well above the median levels for the subclass.

Still, over the last five years, it has underperformed most of its peers in the subclass.

FDG Portfolio Analysis

As I said above, FDG is an ANT ETF, so we only have the June quarter holdings available for analysis. So let us discuss how the ETF's factor exposures might have looked like if all these holdings with the exact same weights were in the portfolio as of August 29. Alas, we do not have any better alternative (we might use the proxy portfolio though, but I believe it will not improve the accuracy of conclusions), as FDG's current holdings are classified.

So as of June 30, in the FDG portfolio, we see 36 common stocks and ADRs (foreign exposures include Belgium, Canada, Denmark, Japan, and Sweden). The portfolio is quite top-heavy, with just two holdings, namely NVIDIA (NVDA) and Amazon (AMZN), two bellwethers I am not surprised to see among the main holdings of a growth ETF, accounting for 27.36% of the net assets.

Company Ticker Sector Weight (as of June 30)
NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) Information Technology 17.26%
Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) Consumer Discretionary 10.10%
Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) Communication Services 9.99%
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) Consumer Discretionary 6.08%
Netflix, Inc. (NFLX) Communication Services 4.99%

Data from the ETF

At the same time, the ten smallest positions (excluding cash & cash equivalents), including Palantir (PLTR), Verisk Analytics (VRSK), etc., accounted for just 7.6%.

Most stocks FDG favors can also be found in the IWF portfolio: 26 of FDG's holdings (86.2% weight) had a 45.2% weight in IWF as of August 27 (and 44.9% as of June 4).

How does its sector mix compare to IWF's?

Despite being quite heavy in IT, FDG is still significantly underweight in this sector compared to the Russell 1000 Growth ETF. Another essential difference is that it is overweight in consumer discretionary, communication, and health care. Besides, as of June, FDG saw no opportunities in materials, real estate, and utilities.

So what factor story is FDG offering? Again, we are talking about approximations and hypothetical exposures.

The main takeaway here is that the FDG portfolio is much more expensive than that of IWF, growthier, and has a higher weighted average beta.

  • With 47.8% of the net assets allocated to trillion-dollar bellwethers, FDG still has a lower WA market cap than IWF.
  • Nevertheless, all valuation indicators I used signal it is priced at a premium to the IWF portfolio. The adjusted earnings yield is more compressed, while P/S and adjusted EV/EBITDA (triple-digit figures were removed for both ETFs) are higher.
  • From a GARP perspective, IWF is stronger, as it has a lower Price/Earnings-to-Growth ratio of 1.55 vs. FDG's 1.76, thanks to its lower adjusted weighted average P/E and higher forward EPS growth rate.
  • However, FDG still has higher WA forward revenue and EBITDA growth rates, and argenx SE (ARGX), which IWF has no exposure to, has solidly contributed to the former, plus a massive 75.72% allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Growth grade or higher and larger exposure to momentum stocks.
  • Even though FDG's profitability and capital efficiency indicators like WA ROA are weaker than IWF's, they are still on solid levels. So I would say this is a high-quality portfolio.

AUM, Liquidity, Expenses

  • As of August 29, FDG has a quite considerable AUM of $348.16 million, which is 2.5x larger than the median for the subclass, as per my calculations.
  • With a C+ rating, its liquidity is neither particularly strong nor especially weak.
  • Compared to passively managed growth-centered ETFs, FDG's expense ratio of 45 bps is quite high (SCHG and IWF have 4 bps and 18 bps, respectively), though still adequate considering its active strategy.

Final Thoughts

So the bottom line today is that, like any other too concentrated growth-focused ETF, FDG is a suboptimal choice for the long term. I like its recent performance, especially compared to the subclass. But with returns for a few years factored in, including periods of acute market stress like 2022, its results fade compared to the S&P 500 and simpler alternatives like QQQ.

So while FDG deserves to be added to a growth investor's watchlist, I cannot find an argument persuasive enough to justify a Buy rating.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook: U.S. Non-Farm Payrolls, U.S. ISM Services PMIs And Eurozone Inflation
data.description:
By Elior Manier

We are concluding a fairly muted trading week, with participants usually taking the final trading week of August to reload their batteries before entering the volatile final four months of the year.

As a matter of fact, the session close will be essential to watch as month-end flows tend to move markets quite largely.

Indecisive trading, Ukraine-Russia talks take a step back

Markets decided to consolidate on relative low-volume trading. Action in Forex markets was almost nonexistent, with low volatility and volumes ranges, while equities went higher step by step.

The S&P 500 still reached some new all-time highs, and the Dow Jones is holding above its previous record. Is a double top into work for the index? This could have big technical implications. It isn't shocking to get timid action ahead of Non-Farm Payrolls, particularly with the current state of things in markets.

Jerome Powell is changing his tone, the Federal Reserve's independence is getting challenged harshly, and diplomatic advances are failing: The German Chancellor Merz announced that Zelenskyy-Putin talks won't take place anytime soon.

With the past month's weak NFP report and a contradicting July PPI putting back tariff fears on the table, the path ahead is unclear yet again.

Weekly performance from different asset classes

Cryptocurrencies have seen quite a selloff this week, but this comes after a decent past week. Nothing is too shocking here as volatility decreases and key data is expected next week. However, some relatively weak highs have been formed, as can be seen in our past week's analysis of the cryptocurrency market.

Indeed, Bitcoin has formed a double top and is failing to provide much to counter its effect, while Ethereum peaked at a new all-time high on Saturday before correcting further. Ether has better prospects than its older brother, but its performance will still be contingent on BTC's performance.

For the rest, Nasdaq and other stock indices weren't performing much, but Nvidia earnings brought positive sentiment back.

The winners of this week are commodities, with oil and gold standing on top (silver also had quite a strong performance). It seems that Powell's pivot, although not having a big influence on other markets, helped metals get back on their 2025 bull train.

We should get a better idea if that is to continue after next week. Always keep an eye on the US dollar to gauge the state of other markets (a strong USD usually leads to lower demand for metals).

The Week Ahead: US Non-Farm Employment in focus (with some other key data points)

The beginning of next week should see volumes slightly getting back due to the beginning of the month and quarter positioning. However, the entire trading planet is awaiting for Friday's Non-Farm Payrolls report; therefore, trading won't be as clear before that date.

Asia Pacific Markets: Growth data for Australia with Governor Bullock and PMIs for China

China and Australia will be in focus for APAC trading next week:

China opens the week with Caixin Manufacturing PMI on Sunday evening, a key gauge of factory activity that often sets the tone for Asia (and has high influence on AUD and NZD performance).

The Services PMI on Tuesday evening will complement the picture, amid a still slowing Chinese economy (most of the recent growth has been generated from government stimuli).

Australia's week is also packed with data: manufacturing PMIs and Q2 GDP land on Tuesday, giving insight into growth momentum. Later, Thursday's Trade Balance figures and comments from the Royal Bank of Australia Governor Bullock will add spice, with markets parsing every word for future policy hints. The RBA has just about one extra cut priced in for the year.

Japan and New Zealand won't see much in terms of key data. The Bank of Japan is the next APAC central bank to release their rate decision, with a pause mostly expected, but either way, the decision will not come before the middle of the month (September 18th).

The BoJ is trying to push back their hike (13% chance of a hike priced in for the year) and would, like US President Trump, love for the Fed to cut to reduce the high interest rate differentials.

In terms of Asian equity markets, the Nikkei and Hang Sang are both consolidating around their yearly highs in search of further momentum, while the Singapore STI, which reached a new record of 4,272 on Wednesday, 13th, has started to decline the past two days - is it profit-taking again or is Asian economic activity starting to get dragged by the Trump tariffs?

US, Europe and UK Markets: US NFP and Services PMIs, Eurozone Inflation and Canadian Employment

The week kicks off slowly for markets but really starts to pick up on Tuesday with Eurozone inflation data. Core CPI is expected to stay soft at 2% YoY, while monthly numbers remain flat. With European inflation hanging around 2%, the European Central Bank should hold still for a while now.

A very small 10 bps cut premium is priced in for the rest of the year, but except for a sudden fundamental change, the ECB main policy rate should stay between 2% and 2.15%.

In the UK, attention turns to Friday with retail sales (expected +0.4% MoM); a release that could give the Pound some direction after a quiet stretch. A weak number would reinforce the idea that consumer demand remains fragile, while a beat may push back some dovish expectations from the Bank of England.

For North America and actually for all markets, the spotlight is firmly on the US labor market. Thursday's ADP Employment Change will provide a first look, before Friday's Nonfarm Payrolls report sets the tone. Markets expect around 78k jobs added - downward revised expectations after the prior month surprise revisions.

The US unemployment rate is still only at 4.2%! Alongside payrolls, average hourly earnings will be crucial to gauge wage pressures. Less influential but released at the same time, Canadian employment data (including unemployment, currently at 6.9%) could bring extra volatility to USDCAD.

Don't forget the Canadian Trade Balance data, releasing on Thursday at 8:30 A.M. Finally, don't overlook PMI releases across the board - ISM Manufacturing on Tuesday, ISM Services on Thursday - all of which will feed into the global growth narrative.

Safe trades and enjoy your weekend!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Franklin Templeton ClearBridge Mid Cap Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: The second quarter was a rollercoaster ride for mid cap stocks, as double-digit declines in April driven by tariff and regulatory changes were more than made up for in May and June. Mid cap stocks broadly advanced, with the Russell Midcap Index returning 8.5%, in line with the small cap Russell 2000 Index but trailing the large cap Russell 1000 Index's return of 11.1%. Easing macroeconomic fears allowed the Russell Midcap Growth Index to return 18.2%, more than 1,200 basis points higher than the Russell Midcap Value Index.
  • Contributors: Holdings in APi, Rubrik (RBRK) and Chewy. Stock selection in the industrials, consumer staples and utilities sectors.
  • Detractors: Holdings in Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE), Avantor (AVTR) and ICON (ICLR). Stock selection in the financials, health care and materials sectors.
  • Outlook: One of the major areas of focus is within IT, where we are hard at work determining who will be the relative winners of the AI investment cycle. With the Russell Mid Cap Index rebalance lowering the weight of the IT sector in our benchmark, it becomes even more important to adhere to our investment philosophy and process to ensure that we capitalize on attractive opportunities while avoiding the losers.

Performance Review

  • The Fund outperformed its benchmark for the second quarter, lifted by solid stock selection in the industrials sector and several strong performers within our IT holdings.
  • Our industrials holdings rebounded nicely in the second quarter, led by APi Group, which specializes in fire protection and infrastructure maintenance. Strong quarterly earnings bolstered performance. The company's strategy of using inspections to help cross-sell products and maintenance is a proven and successful business model that has allowed APi Group to take market share while growing pricing power and margins.
  • Several of our IT holdings also had a strong second quarter including Rubrik, which provides data protection and security services, and a relatively new holding, Microchip Technology (MCHP). Microchip Technology, which supplies analog and mixed-signal microprocessors, gained on the recovery of several of its end markets, and recouping of some lost market share. Microchip is a good example of the type of attractive cyclical self-improvement story that we look for.
  • In financials, private credit manager Blue Owl sold off alongside its peers over increasing uncertainty about the credit cycle as delinquencies ticked upward, and shares in payments company Corpay declined following the April tariff announcements as a substantial portion of the company's earnings come from corporate cross-border payments.
  • Health care proved an obstacle, as companies including ICON and Avantor continued to battle concerns about regulatory policy and an anemic funding environment for biotech companies. However, both holdings are industry leaders within the life sciences industry and we believe their underlying fundamentals remain attractive.
  • We were particularly active in repositioning our health care exposure during the quarter, taking advantage of some of the policy uncertainty-induced declines to add several high-quality names in medical devices such as Penumbra (PEN), which manufacturers thrombectomy products and Insulet (PODD), a developer of insulin pumps.
  • We exited several companies during the period including Entegris (ENTG) and Keysight Technologies (KEYS) in IT. Although semiconductor materials and solutions company Entegris remains a solid business, we believe other semiconductor stocks like Microchip Technology and Qorvo (QRVO) offer better risk/reward tradeoffs. Design and test solutions provider Keysight, meanwhile, reached our fair valuation target during the period.

Outlook

  • The first half of 2025 was a memorable one, to say the least. We grappled with the continued war in Ukraine, another war in the Middle East, a trade war, DOGE, an unclear inflation picture, a rising deficit, a falling dollar, Trump vs. Powell, oil price volatility, the One Big Beautiful Bill, tax policy changes and volatile interest rates, all of which factored into the S&P 500 's (SP500), (SPX) fall from over 6,100 to below 5,000, only to rebound to a new high in June. Despite all this, we remain steadfast in our focus on high-quality companies with strong balance sheets, attractive cash flows and compelling growth prospects.
  • One of the major areas of focus is within IT, where we are hard at work determining who will be the relative winners of the AI investment cycle. With the Russell Mid Cap Index rebalance lowering the weight of the IT sector in our benchmark, it becomes even more important to adhere to our investment philosophy and process to ensure that we capitalize on attractive opportunities while avoiding the losers.

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Drops Amid Worsening Economic Views
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Worsening perceptions of the economy led to the first drop in consumer sentiment in four months. The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index fell by 3.5 points, or nearly 6%, to 58.2 in August. The latest reading was lower than the forecast of 58.6. Consumer sentiment has fallen by 14.3% (9.7 points) compared to a year ago.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regards to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month: a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment confirmed its early-month reading, moving down about 6% from July. Sentiment now stands about 11% above readings from April and May but remains at least 10% below 6 and 12 months ago. This month’s decrease was visible across groups by age, income, and stock wealth. Moreover, perceptions of many aspects of the economy slipped. Buying conditions for durable goods subsided to their lowest reading in a year, and current personal finances declined 7%, both due to heightened concerns about high prices. Expectations for business conditions and labor markets contracted in August as well. That said, expectations for personal finances held steady this month, albeit at relatively subdued levels relative to a year ago. This month, few consumers spontaneously mentioned the recent events at the Bureau of Labor Statistics and the Federal Reserve (interviews closed on Monday, August 25, the day Trump announced he was firing Governor Cook).

See the chart below for a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We've highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession and included a callout to the most recent 12 months. At 58.2, the current level is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put today’s report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 31.0% below its average reading of 84.4 (arithmetic mean) and 30.1% below its geometric mean of 83.2, based on data dating back to 1978. The current index level is at the 3rd percentile of the 572 monthly data points in this series.

This indicator is somewhat volatile, with an average monthly change of 3.1 points. The latest data shows a 3.5-point change from the previous month. To visualize the volatility, here’s a chart with monthly data and a three-month moving average. The bottom half of the chart includes real GDP, which allows us to evaluate the correlation between the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index and the broader economy.

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. To provide additional context, I’ve included a chart that shows the presidencies over our timeframe. As you can see, sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

In August, the CECI fell for the first time in three months to 61.7. This represents a 9.3% decline from the previous month and a 0.7% rise from a year ago. This reading was above the forecast of 60.9.

Meanwhile, the CEI fell for a second straight month to 55.9. This represents a 2.1% decline from the previous month and a 22.5% fall from one year ago. This reading was below the forecast of 57.2.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations moved up from 4.5% last month to 4.8% this month. This rise was seen across multiple demographic groups. Independents and Republicans both exhibited month-over-month increases; expectations for Democrats were unchanged from July. Long-run inflation expectations edged up from 3.4% in July to 3.5% in August. This month ended two consecutive months of receding inflation for short-run expectations and three straight months for long-run expectations. Still, both readings remain well below the highs seen briefly in April and May 2025.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions, while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB business optimism Index (monthly update here).

ETFs associated with sentiment include the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: S&P 500 Earnings: S&P 500 EPS Growth For 2025 Has Risen From 8% To 10% Since Late June
data.description:
Pretty hard to believe, given what we were facing in early April '25 with “Liberation Day,” but the estimated S&P 500 EPS growth has risen from 8% in late June '25 to 10% by late August ’25. It doesn't seem like a lot, but it’s a bigger move than you’d think in 8 weeks' time.

Rates of change:

Since late June '25, all three S&P 500 EPS estimates for calendar ’25, ’26 and ’27 have moved higher, and not by a little bit. The “rates of change” are all improving across most time series. It’s even more unusual to see the forward years like ’26 and ’27 moving higher at this time; the normal pattern is to be seeing slight revisions lower.

Forward 4-quarter estimates:

  • 8/31/25: $283.34 (end of month is Sunday, so that date is being used)
  • 8/22/25: $283.03
  • 8/15/25: $282.02
  • 8/8/25: $282.57
  • 8/01/25: $281.15
  • 7/25/25: $281.37
  • 7/18/25: $280.83
  • 7/11/25: $280.60
  • 7/03/25: $279.92
  • 6/27/25: $269.60

In this weekly progression of the forward 4-quarter estimate, there has been only one week – the last week of July to the first week of August – that the sequential FFQE actually declined from the previous week’s estimate.

(Remember, the forward 4-quarter estimate covers the period from Q3 ’25 through Q2’26, so yes, it’s a leading indicator for S&P 500 earnings, while the vast majority of the data is backward looking.)

Looking forward by sector:

Technology and communication services are driving all the growth (or most of it anyway) in the S&P 500. Look at the change in expected calendar ’25 EPS growth rates for technology and communication services since June 27, ’25. Next year – 2026 – is the bottom half of the above table, and it’s still pretty stable.

Summary/conclusion

Haven’t done a weekly update in a while, even though the numbers were updated every week.

It’s hard to describe how strong this quarter’s results were. The S&P 500 EPS “upside surprise” is still elevated at 7.8%. What’s maybe more interesting is that the S&P 500 revenue upside surprise is 2.5%, which is well above 2023’s and 2024 numbers, even though the S&P 500 returned 25% each of those years.

Makes you wonder if there is a disconnect with the labor market weakness that seems to be talked about so much. Granted, DOGE layoffs, immigration losses and AI job reductions will matter, but only immigration seems to be in full force right now.

Next week, the August nonfarm payroll report is expected to show job growth less than 100k for both the overall and private sector payroll growth. Here’s the economic calendar from Briefing.com for the week of 9/1/25:

The nonfarm payroll data is at the bottom of the page.

It feels like we are in uncharted waters with such strong Q2 ’25 GDP revisions and S&P 500 earnings growth and yet talking about the Fed lowering the fed funds rate, even though the 3-month, 1-year, 2-year, 5-year and 10-year Treasury yields are currently below the fed funds rate of 4.375% and have been for a while.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. All EPS and revenue data for the SP 500 is from LSEG.com. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Intapp: A Buy With A Strong Caveat About Insider Selling
data.description:
Thesis

I rate Intapp, Inc. (NASDAQ:INTA) as Buy, with an expected 18% upside over the next 12 months, based on reasonable assumptions.

The positive aspects of the thesis are: the company possesses several competitive advantages, including its vertically-integrated products that are highly specialized for different industries. Despite there is some deceleration in revenue growth, the company still has room to grow at a more modest rate. The industry and the addressable market have a strong outlook. Additionally, the company has demonstrated good performance in key metrics like NRR and ARR, and its rapidly improving margins open the door for significant EPS growth ahead.

However, there are some negative issues to consider. Insider selling, fierce industry competition, and stock-based compensation (at 17% of total revenues) are all potential concerns. The auditor, despite giving a 'clean rating', noted a 'Critical Audit Matter'.

Business Overview and Latest Results

Intapp, which stands for 'intelligence applied', is a vertically integrated AI-powered company that offers different kinds of solutions. For instance, DealCloud is a CRM with additional features. Regarding compliance, it offers several solutions such as Intapp Conflicts, Intapp Intake, Intapp Terms, and Intapp Walls. Also, it has apps to bill and to track time (Intapp Billstream and Intapp Time), as well as collaboration and workplace solutions.

Additionally, the company differentiates itself by offering 'tailor-made' products highly specialized for specific industries such as legal, investment banking, private capital, and, most recently, real estate, following their recent acquisition of TermSheet on April 21. This high degree of specialization allows them to be more competitive than generic products of companies like SAP or Salesforce.

Regarding their segments, we note several things in the table below. The company is in a healthy transition to a SaaS-type. At the end of the 2023 FY, the SaaS segment represented just 56.2% of total revenues, and now, at the end of the 2025 fiscal year, the same segment grew to 65.8% (rounded to 66%) of total sales. This is positive because subscription revenues are more predictive than license revenues. Also, the SaaS segment is the main one to watch, because it is and will be the most important driver of value, in my opinion.

Second, while there has been some deceleration in growth Y/Y, both in revenues and in gross profit. Lastly, in third place, there is a slight improvement in margins, both in SaaS margin and in gross profit margin.

When evaluating key metrics, Net revenue retention (NRR) may be the most important. As a rule of thumb, SaaS companies should have an NRR of at least 100%; a level below that could suggest that future growth may be jeopardized.

The company's NRR of 120% demonstrates strong performance compared to its benchmarks. Scalecrush states the following:

"The average net retention rate for all SaaS companies in 2023 is 102%, which is the same as the previous year. To achieve a median growth rate of 34%, companies should aim for a net retention rate of at least 100%."

Additionally, according to SaaStr, the top NRR companies range between 120% and 140%. Therefore, Intapp should be rated in the 'Tier 1' category for this metric.

Total annual recurrent revenue (ARR) grew 22.4% Y/Y on FY24 and 20.1% FY25. Cloud-based ARR grew 33.3% and 29.4% Y/Y over the same two periods, respectively.

On August 7, the company's board authorized a new $150mm worth stock repurchase program, which represents around 7.9% of the current market cap. So, we should expect an increase in EPS by around this percentage, assuming all other variables remain constant.

Outlook, Drivers, and Industry

For the next fiscal year, the company's guidance projects total revenues between $566.7mm and $570.7mm, reflecting a 12% to 13% Y/Y growth. This guidance also includes Non-GAAP diluted EPS of $1.09 to $1.13, which would correspond with a P/E ratio of roughly 42x. From another angle, this implies a net margin expected between 15% and 15.7% (Non-GAAP).

The drivers for value creation, and especially for sales, that management puts forth are: getting new clients, expanding through existing clients (up-selling and cross-selling), and the gradual migration of industries to cloud-based systems. Also, they plan to develop new applications and solutions, enter new geographies and new sub-verticals, and pursue strategic partnerships and acquisitions.

This seems to be a sound strategy. Regarding the industry, there are two key things to be aware of, one positive and the other negative. The positive aspect is the double-digit growth expected for the industry; the negative aspect is the high and fierce competition.

Regarding the industry, the Professional Services Automation (PSA) Software industry is expected to grow at a CAGR of 11.9% from 2024 to 2030, according to GrandView Research, and at 11.32% according to Mordor Intelligence.

Valuation

Looking at valuation ratios, we may note that the stock is trading above the median sector. However, maybe one of the most critical ratios is P/E, and as I stated before, both management and analysts are expecting an EPS of $1.11~ for the next fiscal year (which in this case corresponds to the next 12 months). That gives us a forward non-GAAP diluted P/E ratio of around 40-42x.

Below, we can see the potential improvement of expected EPS relative to its prior quarters. Also, it's worth noting that we are going to find a wide range of EPS numbers, due to how they are being calculated. Especially when comparing GAAP vs Non-GAAP/Normalized and Basic vs Diluted. Keep in mind also the repurchase program that will affect EPS by roughly 7%.

Regarding absolute valuation, the model's main assumptions are: a 10% CAGR for sales growth over the next 10 years, and a net margin that will reach 22% by the tenth year. As previously discussed, management and WS analysts expect an implied profit margin of 15% (Non-GAAP) for the next fiscal year. Also, I assume a normal P/E ratio of 25, which is slightly above the technology sector's historic average of 24x. I believe this company should be slightly above the average, as I discussed previously in the NNR metric.

Based on these assumptions, the model suggests an upside of 18% over the next 12 months. The sensitivity analysis below shows how other alternative assumptions would affect the model's valuation.

Risks and Concerns

The main risk is competition. They're competing partially against giants like Salesforce and SAP, but maybe it has not been a 'direct competition' since these companies have more generic (horizontal-integrated) products, while Intapp's ones are more specialized (vertically-integrated). But now, they are in their own way to acquire and develop more vertically-integrated solutions, such as Salesforce Industry with their acquisition of Vlocity in 2020. SAP, on the other hand, launched BTP in 2021.

Additionally, there are other minor but more "direct" competitors, which specialize in the same industries as Intapp, such as Aderant, Elite from Thomson Reuters, and Litera, to name a few. Also, as we all know, the software industry is changing almost every day, and the threat of new competing products is latent.

The second concern is insider selling. Over the past six months, insider ownership has decreased by 9.12% (912 basis points) according to Finviz, bringing the total stake to 9.93%. These transactions include the CEO's and CFO's.

This trend is concerning for two main reasons. The first is the magnitude of the reduction (a 9% decrease in six months). Second, the selling is not limited to one or two individuals, but involves at least six key insiders selling within the same month.

We also note that they have been steadily selling in the last 12 months. While this may not undermine a trading-buy thesis, it would call a long-term investment thesis into question.

Another concern to note is from the auditor, Deloitte. While the firm issued a 'unqualified opinion' (a clean rating), they noted a 'Critical Audit Matter' due to the high complexity of the company's contracts. Although this is not necessarily a negative and may be common in SaaS firms, it is something to be aware of. As cited in the last 10-K (p.63):

"The majority of the Company’s contracts contain multiple performance obligations. Revenue recognition for contracts with multiple performance obligations requires management judgment, especially in identifying and evaluating the various non-standard terms and conditions in the Company’s contracts with its clients and their effect on reported revenues"

Finally, stock-based compensation [SBC] at 17% of revenues is a point of consideration. While this level may be common for SaaS companies, seeking to "attract and retain talent". For example, SBC for S&P 500 companies averaged 1.2% of revenues, and it was 6.8% for the Nasdaq in 2024. While this doesn't affect the thesis based on diluted EPS, we should know this about the company.

Conclusion

Insider selling is a concern, but they still hold a 9% ownership. Also, despite there is a slight deceleration in revenues and key metrics, the company and industry suggest significant growth ahead.

Regarding relative valuation, the stock's ratios trading significantly above the sector median suggest it may be expensive at first glance. However, earnings will be a key driver in the coming quarters and could justify a higher valuation. This is why I chose an earnings-based model for my analysis.

In conclusion, despite the relevant caveats, I see an appealing 18% potential upside, which leads me to rate the stock as a Buy.

data.title: UTF: 6.9% Infrastructure Yield? You Can Do Better
data.description:
With the Trump 2.0-induced energy transition, rising U.S. power demand driven by AI growth, and half of the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law still unspent, the U.S. infrastructure sector is set for massive growth in the coming years.

Investors, whether aiming for capital returns or income, should seriously consider gaining exposure to the infrastructure industry. If you are reading this investment thesis, you have likely already invested or are seriously considering a position in the infrastructure industry.

ETFs would be particularly useful for capturing potential returns over the coming years as they remove the challenge of stock picking. That said, investors should not invest blindly in any infrastructure fund; meticulous analysis is necessary to make the right decision.

The Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (NYSE:UTF) is one such fund, which I will evaluate here. Although this fund has existed for over 20 years, it may not be the best choice for gaining exposure to the infrastructure sector due to its modest returns, high management fees, poor portfolio construction, and other drawbacks that I will cover in this thesis.

The Basics

According to UTF’s website: “The primary investment objective of the Fund is total return, with an emphasis on income through investment in securities issued by infrastructure companies.”

Page 1 of the fund’s prospectus further states that the fund will rely on fundamental analysis to select companies, implying that UTF is an actively managed fund.

UTF also uses leverage of 29.73% (which is relatively high in my view) and has a yield of 6.91% as of July 31, 2025. Two figures that stand out, not favorably, on their webpage are the managed assets expense ratio at 2.77% and the common assets expense ratio at 3.97%.

As an investor, the key figure is the common assets expense ratio of 3.97% because it reflects the total expenses applied to each share held – money out of your pocket. This number should be deducted from total returns to determine the net return on invested capital.

Performance Analysis

Morningstar numbers indicate that UTF’s total return YTD has been 16.05%. After accounting for the 3.97% expense ratio, this equates to 12.08%. Returns over the past 3 and 5 years were 4.36% and 7.65%, respectively - that's gross returns, not net after management fees and expenses.

Compared to the broader market numbers, represented by SPY with an expense ratio of just 0.095%, UTF’s performance has been weak to say the least.

For investors, this means that allocating to SPY instead of UTF, over the long-haul, would have resulted in a higher account value over the same period. Numbers tell the truth, plain and simple.

A Curious Turn

Let’s broaden our scope and compare the fund’s performance with that of the broader market since UTF’s inception.

A curious anomaly that stands out is that UTF was comfortably beating the market until the start of the pandemic but suffered a mammoth drawdown when the pandemic hit and has yet to catch up.

What happened? Why has UTF been struggling since the start of the pandemic?

If we look at the top 10 holdings of the fund and their respective performance since January 2024, it can be said that management has done a strong job in picking stocks.

Combined, the top 10 holdings have an average return of ~38%. This also explains why the fund performed well last year and outperformed the broader market.

NextEra Energy (NEE) ~33%
NiSource (NI) ~68%
TC Energy (TRP) ~31%
American Tower (AMT) ~13%
National Grid (NGG) ~13%
Duke Energy (DUK) ~36%
PPL Corporation (PPL) ~38%
Southern Company (SO) ~42%
Dominion Energy (D) ~36%
Enbridge (ENB) ~38%

(Note: returns listed below are close approximations of price returns, starting January 2024 until present)

That, however, was not the case in the preceding years. The fund’s shareholder reports provide information on the top 10 holdings for the periods ending December 31, 2021 (page 10), June 30, 2022 (page 7), December 31, 2022 (page 10), and June 30, 2023 (page 8).

One can observe that the top 10 holdings did not change substantially, and the fund held on to significant losers such as Canadian Railways (CNI) and Norfolk Southern Corp (NSC) longer than was prudent.

If we extrapolate that loosely estimated 80% success rate on the entire portfolio of equities, we still don’t have a complete explanation for the fund’s lackluster performance after the pandemic.

I believe the answer lies in the fund’s allocation outside of equities, primarily investments in corporate bonds, including junk bonds - which make 14.89% of total portfolio allocation as of June 30, 2025.

Additionally, according to its prospectus on page 13 the fund may invest, and is permitted to do so, around 25% of its managed assets in securities that are rated lower than ‘BBB-’ by S&P or lower than ‘Baa3’, implying that the fund invests in junk grade securities.

The last few years, since interest rate hikes, have been among the worst periods to be invested in junk-rated fixed income securities, or even investment grade, because high yields on U.S. Treasuries caused institutional and retail investors alike to shift their money to those assets instead of riskier fixed-income options.

An ETF called Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA) has suffered the same fate and, in my view, will likely continue to do so for the foreseeable future.

Income Is A Concern

The fund’s NAV chart since inception is concerning. It started at ~$19 and currently stands at ~$25 - an increase of only about 3.5% over 20 years. Despite this, the fund has maintained its distribution rate at above 6% for the past 10 years.

Given the total returns curve of the fund has been upward sloping since inception this could only mean that the fund has not generated sufficient income to fully support those distributions. In other words, it has relied on capital gains and investor capital to maintain a high yield.

That assertion is further supported by the fund’s Section 19 notices. In 2025, UTF generated sufficient income (over 95% of total distributions) only for the months of March and May, while the remaining months relied on capital and/or capital gains to cover the shortfall in income.

Does Not Diversify

Many investors may assume that a fund with “infrastructure” in its name would provide added exposure to the infrastructure sector while diversifying risk away from a total market fund such as SPY.

That is not the case with UTF, as it demonstrates a beta of 1.03, which is largely in line with the market. If an asset does not show movements decoupled from the broader market, it does not function as a diversification or risk-reduction tool. For more on this concept, refer to this explanation and appendix in this article.

Possible Risk Magnification

The fund’s sector composition shows it is heavily weighted toward the electricity sector with 33.91% of total funds allocated towards electric companies.

Given that AI data centers will be a major driver of energy demand in the coming years, it is reasonable to say that UTF is indirectly exposed to the AI industry.

This exposes the fund to the volatility of the technology sector. Technology is known for higher volatility compared to the broader market - QQQ has a standard deviation of 18.7, while SPY’s is 15.12.

A higher risk profile would not be a concern if returns were stronger - i.e., better on a risk-adjusted basis- but as stated earlier, UTF has underperformed the broader market and, as will be show in the next section, another infrastructure fund as well.

A Better Alternative

Based on the information presented, I believe that is is reasonable to state that UTF appears to be a poorly constructed fund. It is overly concentrated in the electricity sector and using junk-rated fixed income assets. Any alternative should first address those weaknesses.

The Global X U.S. Infrastructure Development ETF (PAVE) meets that initial criterion.

According to its website: “The Global X U.S. Infrastructure Development ETF (PAVE) seeks to invest in companies that stand to benefit from a potential increase in infrastructure activity in the United States, including those involved in the production of raw materials, heavy equipment, engineering, and construction.”

PAVE uses the Indxx U.S. Infrastructure Development Index as its benchmark, while the first page of PAVE’s annual shareholder report notes that the fund aims to full replicate that benchmark and is passively managed.

For those who are looking for a quick rundown of the Indxx U.S. Infrastructure Development Index's construction methodology: the index identifies industries and business segments likely to benefit from increased U.S. infrastructure investment, then includes companies with weights determined by their market capitalization. (Note: hyperlinking the benchmark index’s methodology PDF has been problematic; I advise readers to access it on their website linked above.)

Market cap-weighted indices are among my favorite, in fact my masters thesis was on the subject, because they are an effective way to gain exposure to an industry because each company’s weight reflects its relative importance or contribution to the market. This ensures that the companies you hold within the index mirror the natural structure of the overall market.

Such a methodology results in a portfolio that is heavily weighted towards the capital goods sector, which means weighted towards machinery and equipment used not only in electricity, but also in transport, mining, agriculture, infrastructure and industrial machines. Such diversification within industrials gives the ETF a diversified exposure within infrastructure.

Performance, however, is the ultimate measure, none of what I have mentioned thus far would matter if PAVE doesn’t deliver the results.

A comparison of PAVE’s total return against the broader market and UTF shows that PAVE has outperformed SPY by ~20% since inception and UTF by nearly 100%.

This outperformance comes with a higher beta of 1.32 and a standard deviation of 23.05, versus UTF’s beta of 1.03 and standard deviation of 17.69. However, PAVE benefits from a much lower management fee of 0.47%.

Closing Thoughts

Some may argue that PAVE is not designed as an income fund like UTF. That argument carries less weight, or none at all, when considering that UTF’s distributions have largely been sourced from capital gains.

In practice, an investor could sell PAVE shares for income when needed, which is not materially different from what UTF’s management has been doing since the inception of the fund.

There are, however, scenarios in which UTF could outperform in the coming years

If U.S. GDP grows steadily and interest rates are cut for precautionary reasons, signaling a strong economy. This could improve confidence in fixed income securities beyond U.S. Treasuries, leading to a recovery in the corporate bonds segment of UTF’s portfolio. Based on current trends, though, this does not appear likely in the near future.

UTFs heavy exposure to the electricity industry could helps its cause. But even still concerns around NAV degradation, high expense ratio, and risk magnification due to indirect AI exposure are not going away anytime soon.

As a result, I recommend a hold for UTF. Those who are in the red against the broader market due to UTF's poor performance over the last few years should continue to hold the fund until they see more gains from the fund's overperforming equity portfolio. And those who are looking to enter the infrastructure industry should consider other alternatives such as PAVE.

data.title: Franklin Resources Q3: Fallout From Western Asset Continues
data.description:
I rate Franklin Resources, Inc. (NYSE:BEN) as Hold, for income focused investors who are looking for long-term buy and hold investments. BEN is currently dealing with large net outflows from its Western Asset Management unit due to issues involving its former co-CIO, who received a Wells notice from the SEC and then took a leave of absence.

In my observation, management is busy highlighting other successes rather than dwelling on that situation. Still, the net results of the business declined in Q3, with more damage control in store for in future quarters. In my experience, it is better to leave a company in a wounded state alone, vs. load up on shares, hoping that the worst is over.

In looking at the last 10 years of BEN’s history, the stock has had inconsistent cash flows, relying on acquisitions for growth vs. organic growth. As one of the smaller asset managers, vs. say competitors such as BlackRock (BLK), Fidelity or T. Rowe Price (TROW), BEN does not have the momentum of fast rising stars such as Apollo (APO) that help it overcome the issues with Western Asset.

In the near future, BEN will continue to struggle in my view. Management will likely choose to focus on growth of the markets, rate cuts, and opportunities in alternatives, which are still a small player overall.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock are:

  • Pays a quarterly dividend yielding 5%, going ex-dividend 9/30/2025.
  • Inconsistent distribution payouts, with unreliable income.
  • Over the past 10 years BEN has lost value on a total return basis
  • Q3 showed $17.1 billion in net-outflows from the Western Asset Management division due to the fallout of the scandal. It will continue in Q4, but not at the same rate.
  • 30% of AUM in countries outside the U.S., representing a growth opportunity to expand further internationally.
  • Management communication trying to hide poor results by sectioning off Western Asset Management, which they still own and have to cover the cost of losses.
  • Largest opportunity for growth is in alternatives, given the average high net worth client only dedicates 5% of their portfolio to the sector (should be 30%).

Full disclosure, I own shares of Western Asset Diversified Income Fund (WDI), which currently pays an 11.9% yield. I was concerned about the crisis at Western Asset, but it hasn’t seemed to affect performance at the fund.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock as a Hold (as of 8/29/2025), which supports my rating.

Global Asset Manager - Well Known Brands

Headquartered in San Mateo, California, Franklin Resources, Inc. is an asset management holding company. It’s best known for the Franklin Templeton, Western Asset Management, Legg Mason, Putnam, and Brandywine Global Investment Management brands names.

Through its subsidiaries, the firm provides its services to individuals, institutions, pension plans, trusts, and partnerships. It launches equity, fixed income, balanced, and multi-asset mutual funds through its subsidiaries. The firm invests in the public equity, fixed income, and alternative markets.

Franklin Resources, Inc. was founded in 1947 has additional offices in Calgary; Dubai; Edinburgh; Fort Lauderdale; Hyderabad, India; London; Rancho Cordova, US; Shanghai, China; Singapore; Stamford, US; and Vienna, Austria.

Q3 Earnings Results - Serious Outflows

On August 1, the company declared Q3 earnings, with the following financial highlights vs prior year ago period:

  • AUM declined -2% to $1,611.8 billion.
  • Revenue declined -4% to $1,593.2 billion.
  • Operating income declined -11% to $377.8 billion.
  • Net income declined -19% to $263.4 billion.
  • Earnings per share declined -18% to $0.49 per share.

Overall, the quarter showed losses across all key metrics. While listening to the earnings call, I couldn’t help asking myself, “are we looking at the same numbers?” while the CEO delivered an optimistic prepared statement. She was trying to separate the faulty Western Asset division, as if to say… if it weren’t for this situation, we would be doing very well. While that may be true, the company still has to deal with the fallout of the SEC and DOJ involvement, which is sinking the company every quarter.

Underperformance Vs. the Financial Sector

An investment in BEN of $10,000 over the past 10 years would have lost money as shown in the chart below, including dividends, severely underperforming the sector index as represented by Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF). An investor would have been better putting the $10,000 in cash vs. getting $9,929 back.

Valuation

Over the past 10 years, BEN has distributed a variable dividend, with an outsized special dividend in 2018 of $3.00, in comparison to the current quarterly dividend of $0.32.

Over the past 10 years or more, the annual distribution has been volatile with huge swings from an increase of 376% in one year to a cut of -73% in another. In my view, this inconsistency will keep long-term buy and hold investors away from the stock, while others may try to trade it to time a large payout. The chart below shows the annual distributions.

Competition

In a comparison to peers, BEN has the worst 10-year total return and the highest price earnings ratio.

T.Rowe Price Group, Inc. (TROW) is the largest in this set, which is ranked by market capitalization. Delivering a 4.6% dividend, the highest dividend growth of 9%, the highest ROE, and an admirable 10-year total return of 111%, the stock is attractive.

SEI Investments Company (SEIC) appears healthy, with a minimal 1% dividend, 30% ROE and 98% 10-year total return.

Invesco Ltd. (IVZ) owners of the famous Invesco NASDAQ ETF (QQQ) recently saw its performance turn around beating quarterly estimates, launching 3 new ETFs, increasing dividends, and enhancing shareholder returns through buybacks. I authored an article covering their achievements in “Invesco: Poised for Growth With 3 New ETFs.” Click on the title to read.

AllianceBernstein Holding L.P. Limited Partnership Units (AB) is the shining star of the pack with a 10-year total return of 248%. I authored an article covering their potential in “AllianceBernstein: High Distribution Yield with Growth Outlook.” Click on the title to read.

AllianceBernstein has a high distribution yield, with tax advantaged limited partner structure. The company is expanding through private markets, international opportunities, and capturing growth in high-fee segments, driving AUM and fee base growth.

Outlook

Once BEN can get beyond the Western Asset outflows, the largest opportunity for growth will be driven by their alternatives division. It is estimated in the financial planning community that the average high net worth client has only 5% of their portfolio dedicated to alternatives. The growing consensus among planners is that wealthy clients should be dedicating 30% to alternatives because they can handle the longer lock up periods in exchange for higher expected returns with lower correlation to the stock market.

On August 7th, 2025, President Trump signed an executive order allowing private equity, real estate, cryptocurrency, and other alternatives into 401Ks, a market worth approximately $12.5 trillion in retirement accounts. BEN is actively working on this opportunity for client-defined contribution plans.

The next earnings report for Q4 will be around November 3.

Risks To Rating

One of the largest risks facing the company is hidden costs due to Western Asset. While the worst is probably over, the bleeding of assets out of the tarnished brand continues.

On the other hand, BEN will eventually get out from the crisis and it’s possible that fearless investors who buy on weakness may be rewarded longer term. The company certainly generates enough cash flow to pay a decent dividend. The upside opportunity for placing their alternatives into retirement funds represents a significant expansion of the business.

Bottom Line

I rate BEN a Hold at this time while the company continues to deal with the Western Asset situation. Net outflows damaged its Q3 results, prompting management to call out less outflows going forward. The quarterly dividend yields 5%, going ex-dividend on 9/30.

Over the past 10 years, BEN has declined in total return. The future is expected to pick up as they overcome the current crisis and look forward to selling alternative funds to the $12 trillion dollar retirement industry. Investors are advised to wait longer before starting a position in BEN as they shed their issues and rebalance.
